arc_summary: The introduction front-loads an unusually long M1 (sentences 1–21) that moves from grand centrality claims through definitional territory to a focused assertion about trait- and state-based variation — effectively pre-positioning the gap without naming it. **M2 is absorbed into sentence 22**, which folds the implicit gap directly into a purpose announcement, producing a compressed **M2/M3 fusion** rather than an explicit niche-carving moment. M3 then expands across ten sentences that alternate between model-framing (3A/3B), **pre-announced findings** (3C), and structural road-mapping (3D). The rhetorical effect is confidence-forward: the paper presents itself as delivering a model and evidence, not as responding to a crisis in the field.

move_roles.role: Opens with a bold species-level centrality claim, using contrast with other animals to frame creativity as the defining human capacity — establishing maximum relevance before any scholarly machinery appears.

Transitions to the scholarly record, reporting that creativity has long attracted study and exemplifying one dominant tradition — research on creative geniuses — that the paper will later implicitly move beyond.

Introduces a corrective reframing: creativity is not the property of rare geniuses but inherent to ordinary cognition, broadening the territory to accommodate the 'little c' focus the paper will adopt.
Reports the established little-c / big-C distinction and announces the paper's scope, narrowing the territory to everyday creativity without yet claiming novelty.
Justifies the choice of little-c creativity as the object of study with two explicit rationales, reinforcing the significance of the paper's chosen scope.

Establishes the field's working definition of creativity, refines it through the originality-appropriateness tension, and reports the expert-consensus operationalization — laying the conceptual groundwork the dual-pathway model will build on.

Pivots from definitional territory to the specific empirical landscape of trait- and state-based variation, cataloguing prior findings that the paper will unify — this is the final setup sentence before the implicit gap.

Performs a compressed M2/M3 fusion: announces the paper's purpose (implicitly conceding incomplete understanding of trait/state variation) and simultaneously claims novelty for the dual pathway model and its evidentiary base.
Introduces and names the model's two pathways as the paper's core theoretical machinery, situating it as an integrative advance over prior theorising.
Begins the structural road-map, announcing the sequence of the paper's sections on prior theories, model elaboration, ideation tasks, and measurement.
Pre-announces two specific empirical findings — one for each pathway — signalling that the paper delivers concrete results rather than purely theoretical claims.
Completes the structural road-map by announcing the mood-states application section and the closing discussion, including future-research directions.

sentences.text: Humans have an amazing capacity for creativity.
sentences.note: Opens the paper by asserting the existence and significance of human creativity as a phenomenon worth attending to.
sentences.move_note: Centrality claim opening; broad 'X matters' framing.

sentences.text: While other species use artefacts and sometimes manipulate objects to better fit their needs, our capacity for creativity allows us to fly to the moon, develop DVD recorders, create art and write poems, and generate research hypotheses.
sentences.note: Illustrates the scope of the claim in [1] through contrast with other species and enumeration of human creative achievements. Parent: [1] emph.

sentences.text: Because of its importance to human culture, our survival, well-being, and prosperity, creativity has long been studied (Simonton, 2003a).
sentences.note: Reports that creativity has been an established object of scientific study, with citation.

sentences.text: For example, quite some work has been devoted to understanding the characteristics, development, and career trajectories of creative geniuses (e.g., Csikszentmihalyi, 1996; Galton, 1869; Mumford & Gustafson, 1988; Simonton, 2003b).
sentences.note: Reports a specific line of prior research with multiple citations.

sentences.text: Indeed, when we think of creativity, the creative achievements of brilliant scientists (e.g., Marie Curie, Albert Einstein), of great inventors (e.g., Thomas Edison), or of famous artists (e.g., Emily Dickinson, Pablo Picasso, The Beatles) readily come to mind.
sentences.note: Illustrates the prior claim about creative geniuses being the paradigm case. Parent: [4] tempstat.

sentences.text: Although creativity is sometimes treated as an attribute of a few brilliant minds, it is in fact inherent to human cognitive functioning.
sentences.note: Introduces tension against the 'genius' framing just described, asserting a broader view.
sentences.move_note: Contrast against prior framing but still establishing the territory (broader view of creativity); not yet naming a gap in knowledge.

sentences.text: This is expressed in our flexible use of language, our ability to create and use new mental categories to organise our experiences, and our ability to mentally manipulate objects (Ward, Smith, & Finke, 1999).
sentences.note: Illustrates the claim that creativity is inherent to human cognition with specific examples. Parent: [6] contrast.

sentences.text: A distinction is sometimes made between 'little c' and 'big C' creativity (Gardner, 1993).
sentences.note: Reports an established distinction in the literature with citation.

sentences.text: While 'little c' creativity refers to relatively mundane contributions and everyday creativity, 'big C' Creativity refers to genuinely important discoveries and works of art.
sentences.note: Defines the two terms introduced in [8]. Parent: [8] tempstat.

sentences.text: This paper is concerned mainly with 'little c' creativity.
sentences.note: Announces the scope of the current paper.
sentences.move_note: Scope-delimiting sentence that narrows the territory rather than fully occupying the niche; precedes the justification block still within M1.

sentences.text: The study of this type of creativity is important for at least two reasons.
sentences.note: Asserts the significance of studying little-c creativity; sets up the two-part justification that follows.

sentences.text: First, it is important in day-to-day life: it helps us to adapt to changing circumstances, to solve everyday problems, and to create new opportunities (Runco, 2004).
sentences.note: First of two enumerated elaborations of the importance claim in [11]. Parent: [11] emph.

sentences.text: Second, the same processes that lead to 'little c' creativity may also operate in cases of 'big C' creativity (Ward et al., 1999; see also Guilford, 1950), and the study of 'little c' creativity may therefore contribute to a better understanding of creative genius.
sentences.note: Second enumerated elaboration of the importance claim in [11]. Parent: [11] emph.

sentences.text: Creativity is commonly defined as the production of ideas, problem solutions, and products that are both novel (original) and appropriate (feasible, potentially useful) (e.g., Amabile, 1983; Paulus & Nijstad, 2003; Sternberg & Lubart, 1999).
sentences.note: Reports the standard field definition of creativity with multiple citations.

sentences.text: Originality is considered the hallmark of creativity, and indeed one cannot be creative without being original.
sentences.note: Reports an established view in the field about the primacy of originality.

sentences.text: However, ideas or products that are original but not appropriate would be considered crazy and irrelevant.
sentences.note: Introduces a qualification against treating originality alone as sufficient for creativity.
sentences.move_note: Qualifies the territory definition; still within M1 as it refines what IS known rather than naming a gap.

sentences.text: One problem is that original ideas that are considered inappropriate at some point in time may be seen as appropriate (and therefore highly creative) later on (or vice versa).
sentences.note: Asserts a genuine complication in the concept of creativity — the time-dependency of appropriateness judgments.

sentences.text: The work of Vincent van Gogh is a good illustration: Van Gogh died a poor man, but his work now sells for millions.
sentences.note: Provides a concrete example of the time-dependency problem raised in [17]. Parent: [17] emph.

sentences.text: Creativity therefore cannot readily be established in an objective way, and several researchers have suggested that a product or idea is creative when relevant experts agree that it is creative (e.g., Amabile, 1982; Csikszentmihalyi, 1999).
sentences.note: Reports the consensus view in the literature on how to operationalize creativity judgments, with citations.

sentences.text: Although humans in general are a creative species, levels of creativity may vary from person to person (trait-based variation) and from situation to situation (state-based variation).
sentences.note: Asserts the existence of meaningful individual and situational variation in creativity, setting up the paper's core focus.

sentences.text: For example, social psychological research has shown that creative performance is influenced by a broad variety of dispositional and situationally induced mood states (see Baas, De Dreu, & Nijstad, 2008, for a review), by external evaluations and rewards (e.g., Amabile, 1979; Eisenberger & Rhoades, 2001), by situationally induced motivational orientation (i.e., approach versus avoidance; Friedman & Förster, 2000), and by the activation of romantic motives (Förster, Epstude, & Özelsel, 2009; Griskevicius, Cialdini, & Kenrick, 2006).
sentences.note: Reports a range of prior empirical findings on factors influencing creativity, with multiple citations.

sentences.text: This article seeks to further our understanding of these trait- and state-based variations in creativity (also see Amabile, 1983, 1996).
sentences.note: Announces the purpose of the current paper.
sentences.move_note: M2 is implicit here — the gap (incomplete understanding of trait/state variation) is folded into the purpose announcement rather than stated separately as nostat.

sentences.text: We present our dual pathway to creativity model (also De Dreu, Baas, & Nijstad, 2008) and examine supportive evidence derived from a number of studies in which participants engaged in creative ideation tasks.
sentences.note: Claims novelty for the model being presented and announces the evidentiary basis of the paper.

sentences.text: The dual pathway model assumes that there are two pathways to creative performance, the flexibility pathway and the persistence pathway, and that psychological states and traits influence creativity through their impact on either of these pathways, or both.
sentences.note: Introduces and names the core theoretical construct — the dual pathway model with its two named pathways — as analytical machinery for the paper.

sentences.text: The dual pathway model builds on and is an integration of previous theorising.
sentences.note: Synthesizes the preceding framing sequence by situating the model relative to prior work. Parent: [24] framedef.

sentences.text: After discussing some of the previous theories of creativity, we present the dual pathway model in more detail.
sentences.note: Announces the organizational structure/plan of the paper.

sentences.text: We then discuss how ideation tasks (e.g., brainstorming and divergent-thinking tasks) may be used to test our model, and how various measures of creativity derived from ideation tasks relate empirically to each other.
sentences.note: Continues announcing the paper's structure and method.

sentences.text: We next focus on the persistence pathway, and show that persistence may lead to creativity even in the absence of flexibility.
sentences.note: Announces a specific finding/section of the paper.
sentences.move_note: Pre-announces a specific result (persistence -> creativity without flexibility); functions as 3C within the article-structure block.

sentences.text: Focusing on the flexibility pathway, we also show that global (as opposed to local) processing modes are associated with cognitive flexibility, but only lead to original ideas when task performance benefits from cognitive flexibility.
sentences.note: Announces another finding/section of the paper.

sentences.text: We subsequently use the dual pathway model to discuss the effects of mood states on creative performance.
sentences.note: Announces another section/application of the model.

sentences.text: We close with a discussion, in which we consider the applicability of the model to other types of creativity tasks, propose a general hypothesis and discuss avenues for future research.
sentences.note: Announces the closing structure of the paper.
